coredata.dc:title: Photoinduced interaction of arylamine dye with carbon quantum dots ensued from Centella asiatica
coredata.dc:creator: Asha Jhonsi, Mariadoss
Kathiravan, Arunkumar
coredata.dc:description: Developing new carbon nano-materials and interaction studies with organic dye molecules are raising interest in the current scenario for photovoltaic applications. Herein, a simple, economical and green method is adopted for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots via hydrothermal process using Centella asiatica as a carbon source (CQDs). The synthesized CQDs were characterized by vital techniques such as atomic force microscope (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV−vis absorption, steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. The results reveal that the synthesized CQDs were spherical shape with an average diameter of ~ 2nm. It shows the emission maximum at 440nm with a quantum yield of 3% and it exhibit broad and excitation-dependent emission spectra, which is a unique property of CDs. Further, fluorescence quenching of an arylamine dye, 2-Cyano-3-(4-diphenylamino-phenyl)-acrylic acid (TCC) with CQDs has also been studied. The quenching mechanism of TCC with CQDs was evaluated by Stern–Volmer equation and the time-resolved fluorescence lifetime studies. The results explored that the dominant process of fluorescence quenching is attributed to electron transfer from excited TCC to CQDs. As a consequence of the work, CQDs can be used as a acceptor material for various emerging fields such as in photovoltaics, photocatalytic water treatment and etc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Centella asiatica
Carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Arylamine dye
Electron transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a) 20nm scale (b) 5nm scale, (c) SEM image and (d) XRD of CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and (b) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs.
(a) Absorption and emission, (b) Excitation dependent emission and (c) time resolved fluorescence decay of CQDs.
Absorption and emission spectrum of TCC in THF solution.

(a) absorption (b) fluorescence spectral studies of TCC by various concentrations of CQDs (0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.08 & 0.1mg/ml) and (c) linear dependence of 1/F0 − F on the reciprocal concentration of CQDs.
Time resolved fluorescence decay of TCC and TCC in the presence of CQDs.
Proposed mechanism for the formation of CQDs from Centella asiatica.
Mechanism of electron transfer quenching process.

head.ce:title: Photoinduced interaction of arylamine dye with carbon quantum dots ensued from Centella asiatica

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B.S. Abdur Rahman University, Chennai 600048, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, B.S. Abdur Rahman University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Centre for Ultrafast Processes, University of Madras, Taramani Campus, Chennai 600113, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Centre for Ultrafast Processes, University of Madras, Taramani Campus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing new carbon nano-materials and interaction studies with organic dye molecules are raising interest in the current scenario for photovoltaic applications. Herein, a simple, economical and green method is adopted for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots via hydrothermal process using Centella asiatica as a carbon source (CQDs). The synthesized CQDs were characterized by vital techniques such as atomic force microscope (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV−vis absorption, steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. The results reveal that the synthesized CQDs were spherical shape with an average diameter of ~ 2nm. It shows the emission maximum at 440nm with a quantum yield of 3% and it exhibit broad and excitation-dependent emission spectra, which is a unique property of CDs. Further, fluorescence quenching of an arylamine dye, 2-Cyano-3-(4-diphenylamino-phenyl)-acrylic acid (TCC) with CQDs has also been studied. The quenching mechanism of TCC with CQDs was evaluated by Stern–Volmer equation and the time-resolved fluorescence lifetime studies. The results explored that the dominant process of fluorescence quenching is attributed to electron transfer from excited TCC to CQDs. As a consequence of the work, CQDs can be used as a acceptor material for various emerging fields such as in photovoltaics, photocatalytic water treatment and etc.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials including carbon nanotubes, fullerenes and graphene have been playing vital role in various fields such as bioimaging, sensors and energy storage [1–5]. In recent times, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are a new class of nanomaterials with a size of < 10nm, have received much attention owing to their good water solubility, excellent photostability, low toxicity and fluorescent properties [6]. A variety of methods have been developed to prepare the CQDs, which are generally be grouped into two main routes, top-down and bottom-up [7,8]. However, hydrothermal method attested as one of the simple and green synthetic route for the preparation of CQDs using natural resources as precursors. Deriving carbon based nanomaterials from harmless natural sources and their interaction with organic molecules is the current area of research interest. For instance, orange juice, pomelo peel, plant leaves, grass, pomegranate and aloe have been adopted as the carbon precursors, affording green and inexpensive entry to CQDs [9–14]. In this work, a facile and green method for the preparation of CQDs from Centella asiatica via hydrothermal treatment is adopted. Centella asiatica is being used as a traditional medicinal herb in Asia for over 2000 years. It is one of the chief herbs for treating skin problems, to heal wounds, for revitalizing the nerves and brain cells, hence primarily known as a "Brain food" in India. The major active components of Centella asiatica are the triterpenoids, including asiatic acid and asiaticoside, other constituents present were phenolic compounds, carbohydrates and aminoacids [15]. So due to the rich availability, harmless nature and presence of carbohydrates and aminoacids, we have selected this plant leaf as a source for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots.

The interaction of dye molecules with carbon based materials are of key importance in energy storage [16], chemical sensing [17], and drug delivery [18]. For instance, incorporation of graphene sheets into TiO2 nanoparticle films used as photoanodes in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs) gave five times higher power conversion efficiencies than those obtained with TiO2 nanoparticle films without graphene sheets [19]. For the first time, Imahori et al. developed the efficient hierarchical electron transfer cascade system on a semiconducting electrode in a bottom-up manner by using rGO sheet to anchoring organic/inorganic hybrid materials [20]. This hierarchical electron transfer cascade system exhibited remarkably high photocurrent generation compared with the reference device without rGO sheets. Kamat et al. reported the photoanodes assembled from CdSe–graphene composites in quantum dot sensitized solar cells display improved photocurrent response (∼ 150%) over those prepared without rGO [21]. On the other hand, Sun et al. reported the opportunities of CQDs uses in light energy conversion and related applications [22].

Based on the context, here we report the interaction of arylamine dye with the synthesized CQDs for the first time. Since, arylamine dyes have received great attention in dye-sensitized solar cells, because of their high molar absorption coefficient and low cost. For interaction studies, we have selected the arylamine dye [2-Cyano-3-(4-N,N-dimethylanilino)-trans-acrylic acid, (TCC), Scheme 1] [23]. The reason to prefer this dye is; this dye has a very simple structure, easy to prepare and the power conversion efficiency is 2.2%. We have demonstrated the molecular interaction of TCC with CQDs using various spectroscopic techniques. The characterization of CQDs is also presented. The quenching property of CQDs on the fluorescence intensity of TCC was evaluated using the Stern-volmer equation and the time-resolved fluorescence studies. As a consequence of this work, TCC-CQDs material can be used for DSC applications and which may open up a new avenue for developing the devices with higher efficiency.

Leaves of Centella asiatica were collected from the nearby village (kelambakkam, Chennai, India). Cyanoacetic acid and 4-(diphenylamino)benzaldehyde were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Other chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade and purchased from LOBA Chemicals (India). Milli-Q water was used to prepare the samples for spectral measurements.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns were recorded on a Rigaku MiniFleXll-C system using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418Å) radiation and a graphite monochromator in the diffracted beam. A scan rate of 1°min−1 was applied to record a pattern in the 2θ = 25° in the range of 2θ = 20–80°. Particle size and morphology of the synthesized CQDs were investigated by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100 with accelerating voltage of 120kV). The TEM specimen was prepared by depositing a few drops of a diluted carbon dot solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by complete evaporation of the water solvent. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) analysis is done by using Agilent 5500. The AFM specimen on a mica surface was prepared as similar to TEM analysis and the non-contact mode was used for the AFM image measurement. SEM measurement is done by using Nova NanoSEM 600 from FEI Company, Netherlands and EDS analysis is with EDAX system. Elemental analysis for the dried CQDs sample is done by Perkin Elmer 2400 CHN analyser. The diameter of the particles, self aggregation behaviour and surface charge is determined by dynamic light scattering method by using Malvern DLS instrument and zeta potential measurement respectively. FT-IR spectra were obtained by using JASCO FT–IR ATR 6300 spectrometer at room temperature in the range of 4000–400cm−1. The samples were placed in a liquid cell between two windows (CaF2). Mirror velocity is 0.3cms−1 and numbers of co-added scans are 4 then total collection times is less than 2min. Absorption spectra were recorded using Perkin Elmer Lamda 25 UV–visible spectrophotometer. The fluorescence quenching measurements were carried out with Perkin Elmer LS 45 spectrofluorometer. The excitation and emission slit width (each 5nm) and scan rate (200nmmin−1) were maintained constant for all the measurements. Time-resolved fluorescence decays were obtained by the time correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique exciting the sample at 400nm. Data analysis was carried out by the software provided by IBH (DAS-6), which is based on deconvolution a technique using nonlinear least-squares method.

The quantum yield (Φ) of CQDs is calculated by using quinine sulphate [24] as a reference compound in which ϕ value is reported as 0.53. For the measurement of ϕ, the optical density of CQDs in water (η = 1.33) is fixed to less than 0.1 at the wavelength of 360nm. Quinine sulphate is dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (η = 1.33). For both the sample and reference, emission spectra were recorded at the same excitation at 360nm. Fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) of CQDs is calculated by using the following Eq. (1)
(1)
Φ
F
=
(
A
R
/ A
S
)
(
I
S
/ I
R
)
(
η
S
/ η
R
)
2
Φ
R
where the subscript ‘S’ refers to the samples, the subscript ‘R’ refers to quinine sulphate, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I is the integrated emission area and η is the solvent refraction index.
The detailed synthetic procedure of TCC dye and characterization data were illustrated in the supporting information (Fig. S1-S3).

Carbon dots from Centella asiatica is synthesized by a simple one pot hydrothermal method. A typical procedure for the synthesis is as follows, 25g of Centella asiatica leaves were grind in to paste by juicer and 20ml of extract from the paste is collected via ordinary kitchen filter and it is placed in a 100ml Teflon coated autoclave. 30ml of Millipore water is added into that and tightly closed and kept in an electrical furnace at 180°C for 8h, then allows the autoclave to cool to room temperature naturally. A dark brown crude solution is recovered from the autoclave and centrifuged at 3000rpm for 15min to remove the larger bulk particles. A clear yellow solution is collected and filtered through an ultra filtration membrane (0.2µm) and washed with dichloromethane in order to remove the unreacted organic moieties present in the solution. The washing is repeated for minimum of three times using a separating funnel and finally the Centella asiatica carbon dots (CQDs) were collected from the top aqueous layer. The resulting homogeneous yellow clear solution is rotovapoured at 60°C to remove solvents and further it is vacuum dried. The obtained brown solid product is highly water soluble which is redispersed in water and stored at 4°C for further use. The attracting feature of this route is that neither any chemicals nor post-synthetic surface passivation is required. Moreover, the starting material can be obtained from a simple natural source and the synthesis method is cost-effective for large scale production.

Proposed mechanism for the formation of CQDs from Centella asiatica ( Scheme 2) involves the carbonization of constituents of Centella asiatica which mainly consist of triterpenes like asiatic acid, asiaticoside and phenolic compounds, polyacetylenes, carbohydrates, and amino acids. In fact, the clear mechanism for the hydrothermal carbonization of green resources has not yet been reported and the possible mechanism is as follows. The chemical structure of asiatic acid and asiaticoside consist many number of carbohydrate which is mainly composed of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen which undergoes hydrothermal treatment, converted into glucose and fructose which further decomposed into different soluble products like furfural compounds. Also the presence of acids, acetylenes, and phenolic compounds in Centella asiatica undergoes polymerisation and condensation in a controlled manner and gives rise to soluble polymers. Formation of aromatic clusters of these soluble polymers leads nucleation of carbon dots.

HR-TEM images in Fig. 1 a demonstrate the CQDs prepared are nearly quasi-spherical in shape and mono dispersed in nature with an average diameter of 2.18nm displayed in histogram (Fig. S4). Fig. 1 b is the HR-TEM image in 5nm scale which discloses the prepared CQDs is almost spherical in shape which provide evidence for the prepared carbon material is a quantum dot. AFM and DLS analysis also reveals that the synthesized CQDs are in nanometer scale (Figs. S5, S6). The SEM image (Fig. 1 c) of CQDs emerge like layered structures and the EDAS (Fig. S7) analysis expose that CQDs is mainly consist of C (52.1%) and O (25.9%) which clearly illustrate that the CQDs are made up of oxygenous carbon structure. XRD pattern of the CQDs is represented in Fig. 1 d, which exhibit two sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28°, 32° and also show small diffraction peaks at 2θ = 41°, 45° and 66° is attributed to carbon composed in a considerably random fashion. This result indicate that the carbon atoms exist as densely packed alkyl chains with graphitic carbons which enlightening the poor crystalline nature of CQDs is due to more number of oxygen containing groups present in the surface of CQDs.

XPS analysis is performed to investigate the composition and surface state of CQDs. The survey scan of XPS spectrum ( Fig. 2 a) exposes two prominent features of C1s and O1s located at 284 and 533eV respectively, which are consistent with the results of EDAS measurements. Fig. 2 b shows the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs. The peaks around 1304cm−1 and 1090cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations [25] of C–O–C. Peak associated with the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (−OH) groups is at 3426cm−1. The characteristic peaks at 2923cm−1, 2853cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of sp2 and sp3 C−H, respectively. The peaks at 1416cm−1 and 1633cm−1 corresponds to the aromatic C˭C stretching vibration. The FT-IR data revealing the formation of unsaturated carbon during the carbonization process and the presence of oxygen-rich groups (carboxyl, carbonyl and hydroxyl) on the surface [26] of CQDs. Zeta potential measurement (Fig. S8) revealed that CQDs has a value of −9.82mV, which indicates the existence of negatively charged functional groups on the surface of CQDs and the surface is hydrophilic in nature which imparts the high solubility of CQDs in water. In Raman analysis (Fig. S9 ) there is no significant information is observed (presence of graphite-like stacking domains will resulting the 2D and 2G peaks). The absence of such peaks indicated that the prepared CQDs have a higher proportion of aliphatic carbon and smaller polyaromatic domains which is further confirmed by MNR analysis. A highly fluorescent nature of the CQDs may result strong photoluminescence background, so Raman analysis which will becomes difficult to identify the characteristic D & G peaks. The NMR spectra disclose the presence of four different kinds of chemical environments in the four different regions as discussed below. In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S10) the regions found are: 1–2.7ppm (for sp3 C–H protons), 3–4.5ppm (for the protons attached with hydroxyl, ether and carbonyl groups), 5.9–7ppm (for the aromatic or sp2 protons) and 7–8.4ppm (for the aldehydic protons). In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S11), four similar regions viz. 25–37ppm (for sp3 carbons and carbons attached with hydroxyl groups), 66–75ppm (for carbons attached with ether linkages), 124–155ppm (for aromatic or sp2 carbons, weak signal intensity, lower proportion) and 179–190ppm (for C˭O carbons) were found. The NMR data are consistent with the literature report [27]. The results obtained from NMR analysis indicates that CQDs contains higher proportion of aliphatic carbon and smaller polyaromatic domains. The characterization results from FT-IR, Raman and NMR were consistent to each other which confirm the structure of CQDs which consist of various functional groups present in its surface.

Fig. 3a shows the absorption and emission spectra of prepared CQDs in which the absorption spectrum exhibits a sharp peak at 280nm is due to π–π* transitions of the aromatic C˭C bond and a broad shoulder at 330nm is due to n–π* transitions in the C˭O bond originate from the surface of CQDs. Moreover, CQDs exhibits maximum emission at the wavelength of 440nm while excitation at 360nm, which is arises from various defects present on the surface sites of carbon dots. The quantum yield of CQDs is found to be 3.4% which is reliable with the literature reported for carbon dots prepared via green routes [26] without post synthetic functionalization. Fig. 3b shows the excitation dependent emission spectra of CQDs. When the excitation wavelength is varied from 300 to 460nm at 20nm increments, it is observed that the emission maxima varies from blue to green region (380–520nm), this bathochromic shift in emission wavelength with decrease in fluorescence intensity is attributed to the different functional groups such as ketonic, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups present in the surface of carbon dots, which is already evident from the FT-IR analysis. The fluorescence decay of CQDs is shown in Fig. 3c. The decay is fitted by a multiexponential function [F(t) = A1 exp (-t/τ1) + A2 exp (-t/τ2) + A3 exp (-t/τ3)] with the lifetimes of τ1 = 0.72ns (35%), τ2 = 2.28ns (41%), τ3 = 7.70ns (24%) and the average lifetime is calculated as τav = 2.86ns. The multiple lifetimes of the fluorescence imply that various emissive states, due to wide range of chemical environments on the surface of carbon dots.

Before probing interaction between TCC and CQDs, it is necessary to understand the photophysics of TCC alone. Hence, steady state and time resolved fluorescence measurements have been performed to understand the ground and excited state properties of TCC. The absorption and emission spectra of TCC in THF is shown in Fig. 4. The absorption of TCC is due to intramolecular charge transfer between amine donor to cyanoacrylic acid acceptor [28]. It is well known that, ground and excited state properties of the ICT based dyes were found to be very sensitive to the solvent polarity. Hence, solvatochromism of TCC has also been performed. The absorption spectra of TCC in various solvents are shown in Fig. S12 a. It is observed that in non-polar solvents, there is no significant spectral shift. But, in polar solvents, there is a significant hypsochromic shift is observed. Generally, deprotonation or hydrogen bonding of the solvent with the carboxylic acid group of the dye will reduce the electron-accepting strength of the acceptor moiety (−C≡N), which in turn significantly weaken the donor–acceptor interaction in the dye, which will lead to a hypsochromic shift in the absorption spectrum [29]. A similar solvatochromic effect is observed in the fluorescence studies (Fig. S12 b). The corresponding absorption and emission data in various solvents were listed in Table S1. The influence of solvent polarity on the optical properties of the TCC can be studied by Lippert-Mataga equation: Δν = ν
abs
−ν
emi
= 2(μ
e
−μ
g
)
2 Δf/hca
3
+ C, this equation describes the interactions between the solvent and the dipole moment of the dyes [30]. In this equation, μ
g and μ
e are the dipole moments in the ground and excited states, respectively, h is the Planck constant, c is the speed of light, a is the radius of the dye and Δν is Stokes shift, which is calculated from the difference between absorption and emission maxima obtained from the corrected spectra on the wavenumber scale. Δf is called the orientation polarizability, which can be expressed as (ε−1)/(2ε+1)−(n
2
-1)/(2n
2
+1), where, ε and n are the dielectric constant and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The Lippert-Mataga plot of TCC is depicted in Fig. S13. It can be found that the plot of Δν against Δf has a positive correlation and is linear with correlation coefficient R = 0.91. The change in dipole moment (Δμ=μ
e
−μ
g ) for TCC have been calculated from the slope of the linear plot and is listed in Table S2. The estimated Δμ value of TCC is more positive and suggestive of a large dipole moment, which confirming the more pronounced ICT character of TCC in the excited state.

Fig. 5 a shows the absorption spectrum of TCC with various concentrations of CQDs. Upon increasing concentration of CQDs, the absorbance of TCC is regularly decreased with hypsochromic shift is observed. This result indicates that there is a strong interaction between TCC and CQDs is existed in the ground state. Like absorption spectra, the presence of various concentrations of CQDs showed a remarkable effect on the fluorescence intensity of TCC, as revealed in Fig. 5 b. The excitation wavelength is 430nm. Although, CQDs shows an emission in the wavelength range of 450–600nm, but this emission is very weak compared to TCC, hence this will not hamper the fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence spectrum of CQDs is given in the inset of Fig. 5 b. Moreover, the emission spectrum of TCC in the presence of 0.1mg/ml CQDs showed no dependence on the excitation wavelengths (at 380, 400, 420 and 440nm). This again supporting that the quenching cannot be influenced by fluorescence of CQDs.

Quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the TCC is analyzed using Stern–Volmer relationship (2):
(2)
I
0
/ I
=
1
+
K
SV
[
Q
]
where, I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of TCC in the absence and presence of the CQDs and KSV is the quenching constant. The plot between I0/I and [Q] is non-linear ( Fig. S14 ). The deviation of I0/I from linearity is due to the presence of static quenching (i.e. ground state complex). The formation of ground state complex as already evidenced in the absorption spectral studies. Hence, static quenching plays a major role in the quenching of fluorescence of TCC by CQDs. So, one can determine the apparent association constant from fluorescence quenching data using Eq. (3),
(3)
1
F
0
−
F
=
1
F
0
−
F
′
+
1
K
a
p
p
(
F
0
−
F
′
)
[
CQDs
]
where, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of free TCC, F′ is the quenched fluorescence intensity and F is the observed fluorescence intensity at its maximum. The plot between 1/(F0-F) and the reciprocal concentration of CQDs gives a straight line [ Fig. 5 c] and from the slope we have calculated the Kapp value and is 22.3 (mg/ml)−1. This value is quite comparable with reported in literature [31], indicates that the strong interaction of TCC with CQDs.

The decrease in fluorescence intensity of TCC by CQDs may be attributed to energy transfer or electron transfer process. The possibility of energy transfer process from TCC to CQDs can be easily ruled out due to the absence of overlap between the fluorescence spectrum of TCC with the absorption spectrum of CQDs. So, the later process (i.e.) electron transfer could be a dominant process for the fluorescence quenching of TCC by CQDs. Hence, stepwise electron transfer mechanism has been depicted in Scheme 3.

The thermodynamics driving force (ΔGet) of electron transfer between TCC and CQDs can be verified according to the well known Rehm–Weller expression (4).
(4)
Δ
G
et
=
E
1 / 2
oxi
−
E
1 / 2
red
−
E
s
+
C
where, E1/2 oxi is oxidation potential of donor (1.21V) [32], E1/2 red is reduction potential of acceptor (−0.72V), Es is the excited state energy of TCC is 2.61eV (Es value is estimated from the intersection of absorption and florescence spectra) and C is the Coulombic term. Since one of the species is neutral, the coulombic term in the above expression is neglected. The calculated ΔGet value is −0.68eV. The obtained ΔGet value is negative and hence the electron transfer reaction of TCC is thermodynamically favorable.

Further, we have employed time correlated single photon counting technique to perceive the fluorescence lifetime of TCC and TCC…CQDs complex. It has been previously established that the fluorophore adsorbed on oxide surface had a significantly shorter lifetime when compared with fluorophore alone and this decrease of lifetime was correlated with an electron transfer process [31,33–36]. Based on the context, we have measured the fluorescence decay of TCC in the presence of 0.05mg/ml CQDs is shown in Fig. 6. In the absence of CQDs, the fluorescence decay of TCC can be fitted to a single exponential function expressed as [F(t) = A exp (−t/τ)] with lifetime of 2.87ns. However in presence of CQDs, the fluorescence decay of TCC is shortened and it is fitted to a bi-exponential decay [F(t) = A1 exp (−t/τ1) + A2 exp (−t/τ2)], with short-lived and longer-lived components. If electron transfer between TCC and CQDs is the process dominating the shorter component in the TCC-CQDs system, then we can estimate the rate of electron transfer from the emission lifetimes (Eqs. (5)–(7)).
(5)
S
*
→
k
r
S
+
h
ν
/
S
*
→
k
n
r
S
⇒
τ
=
1
k
r
+
k
n
r
(6)
S
* …
CQDs
→
k
e t
S
+
+
CQDs
(
e
−
)
⇒
τ
a d s
=
1
k
r
+
k
n r
+
k
e t
(7)
k
e t
=
1
τ
a d s
−
1
τ
where, τads is the lifetime of the short lived component, τ is the long lived component and ket is rate of electron transfer. By substituting the values of 46ps and 0.92ns as the lifetimes of the short and long lived components and we obtained ket as 2.1 × 1011 s−1. The calculated ket is very fast, such an estimate of the faster electron transfer rate provides the basis for using carbon based materials for collecting photogenerated electrons.

In summary, we have developed the CQDs using Centella asiatica as a source without further treatment or modification. The synthesized CQDs are effectively quenched the fluorescence of TCC. The detailed investigations have been carried out using steady-state and time-resolve fluorescence spectroscopy to understand the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching. The spectroscopic results reveal that the quenching of TCC by CQDs is due to electron transfer. Here, TCC act as a donor and CQDs act as an acceptor. The negative value of the free energy change suggests that electron transfer from TCC to CQDs is feasible. These results will provide a fundamental clue for the development of an efficient hierarchical electron transfer cascade system for photovoltaic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M.A.J. and AK sincerely thanks the Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi, India for the DST-SERB Project (Ref. No. SB/FT/CS-125/2013, Dt. 30/06/2014 and Ref. No. SB/FT/CS-316/2012, Dt. 04/12/2012). M.A.J thanks SAIF, IIT Madras, Chennai, for assistance with HR-TEM and SEM facilities. M.A.J also acknowledges Prof. G.U. Kulkarni, Ms. Selvi and Ms. Kiruthika for EDAS and power XRD analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2017.06.056.
